chapters.title: Step one: win a boat and sell it
chapters.text:
Picture an 18 year old who is on the path to medical school. His parents poured everything into his education, and the weight of their hopes sits on his shoulders. The pressure builds, the energy drains, and the plan starts to slip. Then, the night before exams, he lies on his back, staring at the ceiling, unable to crack open a textbook.

Rethink your goals. Are they really yours, or someone else.

That is what happened to Alex Banayan, and it pushed him to write a book that became a bestseller and turned his mind and life around. He was a young man at a dead end, following a script his parents had set, with no idea what he actually wanted. How do people push through that foggy stretch of doubt and insecurity to reach the top. Has Bill Gates ever been stuck like that. If yes, how did he break out. Alex searched through piles of books in the university library and found no answers. So he decided to write his own.

His path took an unusual turn when he made a bold move to fund the idea. Curious about the twist. Alex entered a TV game show without knowing the rules. He leaned on quick thinking and a knack for improvisation to handle whatever came his way. By sheer luck, he won a boat, which he sold for 16,000 dollars and set aside as a fund to interview Gates.

If that teaser hooked you, get ready for a gripping story of grit, cleverness, and chance. You are in for a ride.

chapters.title: Steven Spielberg and the road less obvious
chapters.text:
Alex dreamed of writing a book full of life hacks from the world most successful people. Each contributor had to share a hidden secret or a unique trait that helped them get everything at once. Is there a master key that opens every door. Or maybe it is a password only a chosen few know.

Shaping a dream is already half the battle, but what comes next. There is no playbook for doing something nobody has done before. Many promising ideas fade at this stage because the fear of the unknown paralyzes people. So it helps to stay open to advice and unafraid of asking for guidance. That realization helped Alex find his footing and take the first steps toward interviewing the biggest names and writing a book about them.

Pushing past the awkwardness, he reached out to everyone in his circle, including acquaintances, professors, and family friends. Through those calls, Alex learned that Steven Spielberg would soon appear at a charity event at a local film school. He threw himself into research, reading several volumes of the director biography and watching every film. The prep was essential so he could ask a strong question at the first chance and keep the conversation going.

Find role models and learn from their path to grow your own interests.

A small spoiler. Alex never got to interview Spielberg in person, but the effort was not wasted. He picked up insights about the director life that he treated as a tool for reaching his goal.

In his early twenties, Spielberg had driven to Universal Studios and wandered the lot until he met a studio worker near the parking gate. They chatted, and the worker handed him a pass to the lot. Spielberg spent several days there, making friends and soaking up everything he could. That experience led to his short film Amblin, which won a contract with Universal Studios for the then 23 year old director. That is how he became the youngest director in Hollywood history.

A happy coincidence means little without persistence, self belief, and talent. So it was not luck, but a careful approach.

chapters.title: Step two: build your own dimension
chapters.text:
Inspired by Spielberg, Alex decided to get closer to the people he admired by volunteering at a business conference. Being in the right place at the right time paid off, and Alex landed an interview with Qi Lu, then president of Microsoft Online Services.

Lu was in his forties, dressed plainly, and easy to talk to. He grew up in a poor village in China, where a decent education or a healthy environment felt out of reach. His hometown lacked basics like running water and electricity, and many residents went hungry. At 27, Lu was earning seven dollars. How did he climb out of that situation to lead a company of that scale.

Lu dreamed of attending an American university but could not afford it. He studied computer science at the University of Shanghai instead and looked for ways to stretch his potential. So Lu decided to buy himself time. He figured everyone has the same 24 hours in a day, so he cut his sleep to four hours a night, which worked for him, and freed up an extra four hours a day to study. More than two decades later, the Microsoft Services president still follows that schedule. He wakes at 4 am, starts work at 6 am, and works for 18 hours a day, six days a week. Lu believes consistency is the key to success. His colleagues say he lives by his own clock, Qi time.

Whether cutting sleep is healthy is a personal call. Still, the story offers a sharp lesson. Even in tough conditions, there is always a way to find an edge over the competition.

What happened next in Lu story. Outsiders might call it luck. As a student, Lu wandered into a lecture by a visiting teacher, who offered him a full scholarship to study in the US. After graduation, friends introduced him to Yahoo management, and eight years later he became the company vice president. Soon after, he received an offer from Microsoft. How much of that story is pure luck. The verdict is yours.

A lucky ticket may come your way, but prepare for it as if you expect it tomorrow.

chapters.title: The five rules of the world elite
chapters.text:
By this point, you may have assumed that everything came easy for Alex, and influencers lined up to give him endless interviews. Of course, that was not the case. The opposite was true. Alex got so many rejections that he often sank into despair and thought about giving up. Then he would stumble on a spark of inspiration that brought the energy back.

A reward waits at the end of the road, but you have to survive the sharp turns to reach it.

Alex turning point came when he met Elliott Bisnow, a multi-company owner and founder of the Summit Series, a self help organization for entrepreneurs. In just a few years, Summit grew from 19 to 750 participants, with names like Bill Clinton, Ted Turner, and Sean Parker in the mix.

The more Alex learned about Elliott, the more amazed he was that such a run was even possible. How could an ordinary 25 year old break into the world elite circles, become one of them, and earn money from it. Fortunately, Alex met and talked with his new idol. He had no idea this would turn into a long and close friendship. There was no real reason for it, but Elliott saw an entrepreneurial spark in Alex and predicted a bright future. He put it simply by saying, you are one of us. Elliott let Alex sit in on his business meetings and laid out a few rules that work as universal advice.

Never hold your phone in a meeting. Taking notes on paper is more respectful to the people in the room. Treat everyone the same, no matter their status. If you act like a fan, people will treat you like one. Keep your wins private. Resist the urge to post every success on social media. Let people get curious and come to you. Keep your word and learn to keep secrets. You need to be reliable to be trusted. Stay open to adventure.

Soon, Alex became a full member of Elliott team and picked up the experience and contacts he needed. He was even offered a permanent role as a personal assistant, but for Alex that meant giving up his plan to write the book. Faced with a tough call, Alex stayed loyal to his core dream, an interview with Bill Gates.

chapters.title: Step three: be a living, vulnerable person
chapters.text:
As you can see, intentional effort and lucky breaks go hand in hand, and they often overlap. In Alex life, there was one lucky break for every ten deliberate moves toward the goal, and this stretch was no different. Behind it sat a string of interviews with well known figures and a long list of attempts to lock down a book deal with a literary agent. Inspired sprints were followed by weeks of despair, and now another crisis hit.

It was an ordinary day, and Alex sat outside a grocery store eating a sandwich. Then, in classic American comedy fashion, one of the most famous interviewers in history, Larry King, walked right past him. King had hosted a show on CNN for 20 years. He also lived in the same neighborhood as Alex and had breakfast at the same spot every day. Why had Alex never thought to reach out to him before.

Touched by Alex request for interview advice, King invited the young man to join him for breakfast. The first piece of advice he gave was not to copy those who had already made their dream real. King told Alex not to focus on what and how his idols do things, but on why they do them.

The more precisely you frame your dream and its value, the better you will carry it out.

King also shared how he once knocked on the door of every radio station and introduced himself to the leaders in person. Many years later, even with the internet in full swing, he would still do it the same way. He knew people want to deal with people, not emails, and that you can only build real contact in person. Alex looked at his own experience and realized he was most effective when he met people face to face. So his next stop was a TED conference, where he was supposed to meet Bill Gates chief of staff. Alex felt unsure of himself, since he had already received rejection letters from the Gates office many times. Maybe it was not him who had been refused, but only the letters on the screen. Either way, the only move left was to face the fear.

The fastest cure for nerves is action, as Alex Banayan has put it.

A fun note here is that Larry King has been officially married eight times.

chapters.title: Bill Gates will not hand you the answers
chapters.text:
Of course, Alex got his way. You never doubted the resourceful hero of this story, did you. In the end, the interview with Bill Gates happened.

Alex saw Gates as a superhero and admired not just his mind but also his kindness. Having reached the top of business and used his career to the fullest, Gates could have taken a long vacation and enjoyed the perks of the world, since every material comfort was within reach. Instead, he turned to charity and launched programs aimed at helping tens of millions of people. His work focused on cutting child mortality, stopping the spread of disease, and giving more people access to education.

Naturally, meeting Gates had always felt like the final step in Alex plan to write the book. He expected Gates to share something that would flip his world upside down. He wanted a clear, beautifully designed secret that could change his life. To no surprise, the young man was disappointed. Nobody hands out a universal guide, not even Gates. The big insight he hoped for was not coming.

No one can hand you the most valuable lessons. You have to earn them through experience.

At first, every answer from Gates felt like a trick or a half truth to Alex. He could not understand why Gates would not share real wisdom. It took time before Alex caught on to what the richest man in the world was saying. Gates advice boiled down to this. Stop expecting fast results from anything, because quick wins are rare and never guaranteed. He stuck to long term strategies and put reputation ahead of fast profit. Gates did not hand over tricks for winning the love of millions but talked about the value of building trust through honesty and responsibility.

It took Alex a while to see that conversation in a new light, with the benefit of more experience, and to let go of the idea of picking up life hacks and instant rewards. Each of his idols had walked a unique road, and none of those roads were easy.

Genius is the opposite of expectation, as Alex Banayan has written.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on finding the third door
chapters.text:
Picture success as an exclusive club. You can walk in through the front door, but the line is long. The line is full of people living a linear life on the standard path of study, work, retirement. Their route is steady and predictable. Entry is guaranteed, but for many it eats up their youth and middle years, and some wait in line just to give their children a chance. About 99 percent of people are waiting at that entrance.

On the other side of the building sits the second door. It is the VIP entrance, where people skip the line. That door is only open to those born into billionaire or celebrity families.

That setup feels unfair, but there is another way. Alex called it the third door, a secret entrance that nobody knows about, and you can only find it if you look hard. To reach it, you have to break out of the line, cross the whole block, knock on a hundred doors, climb through a window, drop down a few floors, and sneak past the kitchen.

The world leading thinkers have found their third door. And without realizing it, Alex did too. He set out to write a wild list of life hacks and ended up writing a book about unmet expectations, uncertainty, luck, and failure. In the end, that is the most valuable thing a person can own, a winding road, walked at your own risk, leading to the third door.

So before you give up because everyone around you says your goal is impossible, take a careful look at your surroundings. What looks like a dead end may be a fork in the road. May you find enough adventure, courage, and fire to discover the incredible chance that is waiting.

A few ideas to try next include getting to know the people who inspire you as friends, not fans. Study the biographies of people who have made it in the field you care about and look at how they found their third door. Do the things that scare you, because that experience gives you skills and knowledge you cannot pick up any other way.

If you made it all the way to the end, well done.
